title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
Unit-level SAE models use survey microdata directly. They can be more powerful than area-level models, but they require much stronger data conditions.
For this TRACK-UK tutorial, unit-level SAE is mainly included as theory and context. The practical workflow focuses on area-level models because the available survey and covariate structure is better suited to Fay-Herriot-style estimation.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
1 Area-Level versus Unit-Level SAE








Feature

Individual or household survey records


Auxiliary data
Domain-level covariates
Unit-level covariates for the population or poststrata


Typical model
Fay-Herriot
Nested-error model, GLMM, MRP, empirical best prediction

One estimate per area

Practical burden
Lower

Survey-population mismatch and stronger model assumptions

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
2 Basic Unit-Level Idea
Suppose (y_{di}) is the outcome for unit (i) in domain (d), and (x_{di}) is a vector of unit-level covariates.
A common nested-error model is:
\[
y_{di} =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d + e_{di},
\]
where:
\[
u_d \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2),
\quad
e_{di} \sim N(0, \sigma_e^2).
\]
The domain random effect (u_d) captures unexplained differences between areas. The unit error (e_{di}) captures unexplained differences between people, households, or other sampled units within areas.
For binary outcomes, the model is usually changed to a logistic or probit mixed model:
\[
\operatorname{logit}(p_{di}) =
x_{di}^{\top}\beta + u_d,
\]
where:
\[
y_{di} \sim \operatorname{Bernoulli}(p_{di}).
\]

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
3 Estimating a Domain Mean
The finite-population target for domain (d) is:
\[
\theta_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in U_d} y_{di}.
\]
The sampled units have observed (y_{di}). For the non-sampled population units, the model predicts or simulates (y_{di}) using their covariates.
A simple empirical best prediction idea is:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{EBP}_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\left[
\sum_{i \in s_d} y_{di}
+
\sum_{i \in r_d} \hat{y}_{di}
\right],
\]
where (r_d) is the non-sampled part of the population in domain (d). In practice, many methods simulate values for the non-sampled population repeatedly, then summarise the simulated domain estimates.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
4 Required Data
Unit-level SAE usually needs:

survey microdata with the outcome and unit-level covariates,
population or census microdata with the same covariates for all units, or poststratification cell counts,
comparable variable definitions across survey and population data,
domain identifiers that link the survey and population data,
weights or design information, especially if the survey design is informative,
enough sampled domains to estimate area effects reliably.

The most difficult condition is comparability. If a covariate is measured differently in the survey and census, or if the survey and census refer to different populations or periods, the resulting estimates can be biased.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
5 Why It Can Be Powerful
Unit-level models can:

use more information than area-level models,
estimate indicators for unsampled domains,
produce estimates at multiple aggregation levels,
handle non-linear indicators through simulation,
use individual or household covariates rather than area averages.

This is why unit-level methods are common in poverty mapping and microsimulation contexts.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
section: 6 Why It Is Not the Main Practical Route Here
text:
6 Why It Is Not the Main Practical Route Here
For the current TRACK-UK SAE tutorial, area-level models are more productive because:

the worked survey outputs are naturally prepared as indicator-by-domain files,
Local Authority identifiers are available for the National Survey for Wales example,
population microdata with all required matching covariates may not be available,
routed transport indicators can make unit-level target populations difficult to define,
the aim is to teach a reusable workflow for researchers starting from direct estimates.

Unit-level SAE is still useful to understand. It becomes more relevant if the project later connects SAE with synthetic populations or poststratification cell counts.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
7 Relationship to Synthetic Populations
Synthetic populations and unit-level SAE can work together. A synthetic population can provide a population-like set of units with covariates, and a fitted survey model can predict or simulate outcomes for those units.
However, this introduces additional assumptions:

the synthetic population must represent the target domains well,
survey and synthetic-population covariates must be comparable,
uncertainty from the synthetic population should not be ignored,
model predictions should be validated against direct estimates or known aggregates where possible.

The connection is noted here, but a full synthetic population SAE workflow is outside the present scope.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
8 Practical Pattern
A generic unit-level workflow is:

Harmonise survey and population covariates.
Fit a mixed model to the survey microdata.
Predict or simulate outcomes for the non-sampled population.
Aggregate predicted and observed outcomes to domains.
Estimate uncertainty using bootstrap, posterior simulation, or model-specific MSE methods.
Compare estimates against direct estimates and higher-level benchmarks.


# Placeholder only: final code depends on the selected package and data structure.

# library(emdi)
# ebp_fit <- ebp(
#   fixed = outcome ~ age_group + household_type + car_access,
#   pop_data = population_microdata,
#   pop_domains = "domain_id",
#   smp_data = survey_microdata,
#   smp_domains = "domain_id",
#   weights = "survey_weight",
#   MSE = TRUE
# )

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
9 Caveats

Unit-level SAE is model-heavy and should be checked carefully.
If the survey design is informative, ignoring weights can bias estimates.
If population covariates are weak predictors, estimates can be poor even with many population records.
MSE estimation can be computationally expensive.
Outliers, non-normality, and model misspecification can matter.
The method can give a false sense of detail if the population microdata are synthetic or weakly validated.

title: Unit-Level SAE Models
text:
10 Notes on Sources
The standard unit-level SAE idea is to model the outcome using survey microdata, then predict or simulate outcomes for population units before aggregating to domains. See the unit-level SAE chapter in the poverty mapping guide for a fuller discussion of empirical best prediction, census-based workflows, requirements, and caveats.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text: SAE Toolkit Lite is for quick adaptation of the materials to another survey, geography, or indicator. It assumes an area-level SAE workflow and a Bayesian modelling approach.

title: SAE Toolkit
text:
1 Minimum inputs
You need:

A survey file with outcome, domain ID, and appropriate weight.
Documentation explaining survey design, routing, weights, and target population.
A domain-size table for the chosen denominator.
Covariates available for every target domain.
Optional boundary geometries and adjacency structure for spatial models.

If your inputs do not match this clean setup, use the pitfalls and decision points page before fitting a model.

title: SAE Toolkit
text:

2.1 Survey indicator file

Numeric outcome, often binary 0/1.

Survey weight selected for the estimand.

Optional survey stratum.

Optional primary sampling unit.





2.2 Domain-size file



Column
Meaning

Denominator matching the target population.

2.3 Covariate file







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.

Area-level predictor available for every target domain.

title: SAE Toolkit
text:
2 Reuse workflow

Fill in an estimand register.
Validate the survey weights and routing.
Join domain sizes.
Calculate direct estimates.
Fit a baseline FH model.
Add covariates and spatial structure step by step.
Run diagnostics.
Export estimates with uncertainty and caveats.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
2 Downloadable scripts
Reusable scripts can be adapted for another survey once the input files follow the schema above.



Script
Purpose




direct_estimates.R
Calculate direct estimates by domain, sampling variances, and confidence intervals.


covariate_sae.R
Fit Bayesian area-level models using direct estimates, sampling variances, and area-level covariates.


spatial_sae.R
Build adjacency or spatial weight matrices from domain boundaries and fit Bayesian spatial SAE models.


diagnostics.R
Create comparison tables and plots for checking estimates, uncertainty, and model outputs.

title: Practical Example
text:
This page uses the National Survey for Wales (NSW) data preparation workflow to show how estimands, domain sizes, and weights are defined before direct estimation.
The NSW data preparation notebook creates binary indicators from the survey and attaches a Local Authority code, la_code, to each respondent. In this worked example, the target domain is therefore Local Authority.
The choice of domain is partly driven by what spatial information is available. Here, the survey data and the external population tables are available at Local Authority level. In practice, if survey respondents have more granular spatial identifiers, such as LSOA, MSOA, or postcode-derived zones, the choice of domain can be more flexible, provided matching domain sizes and covariates are also available.

title: Practical Example
text:
1 Indicator Families
The NSW preparation workflow includes indicators from several families:

household car access,
commuting mode and commuting distance,
hybrid and remote working,
bus and train use,
bus and train satisfaction,
walking and cycling frequency.

The examples below focus on three indicators that illustrate common estimand choices.

title: Practical Example
text:
2 Example 1: Households Without Car Access
Notebook indicator:

no_car = 1 when CarUse indicates no access to a car.

Estimand:

Estimate the share of households without access to a car in each Local Authority.








Component
NSW example




Outcome
Household has no access to a car


Target population
All households


Target domain
Local Authority


Domain size
Number of households in each Local Authority, household_total


Weight
Household grossing weight, scaled from SampleHhWeight


Interpretation
“x% of households in this Local Authority do not have access to a car.”



This is a household estimand, so the denominator should be households, not adults. The downstream direct estimate should therefore use a household weight and a household domain size.
Examples:







Estimand
Indicator construction




Share of households without access to a car

Share of adults who use the bus weekly
pt_bus_use = 1 if BusFreq12M = 1


Share of adults who commute by car
commute_car = 1 if commute mode is car; non-commuting adults may be coded as 0 if the estimand is adult prevalence


Share of commuters who commute by car
commute_car = 1 if commute mode is car, but only commuters are in scope

title: Practical Example
text:
3 Example 2: Car Commuting
Notebook indicator:

commute_car = 1 when TravWkModeAll4 = 1 or TravWkModeLong = 4.

This survey question is routed through work and commuting questions, so the target population must be chosen carefully.

The NSW notebook uses the first version for the current workflow: all adults aged 16+ as the target population, pop_16plus as the domain size, and WalesAdultWeight as the weight. That makes the indicator an adult-population prevalence measure, not a proportion among commuters only.
If the aim changes to estimating the share of commuters who use a car, the domain size would need to change to the number of commuters in each Local Authority. If that denominator is not available, the direct estimation method or spatial resolution may need to change.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
Direct estimates are the first estimation step in an area-level SAE workflow. They show what the survey can say before borrowing strength from covariates or spatial structure.
In R, this step can be implemented with the sae package, using sae::direct() to compute direct estimates and their sampling variances under common survey-design settings.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Notation
For domain (d):

\(s_d\) is the set of sampled units in the domain.
\(n_d\) is the number of sampled units in the domain.
\(N_d\) is the known population size or domain size.
\(y\_{di}\) is the outcome for sampled unit (i).
\(w\_{di}\) is the survey weight for sampled unit (i), often (w_{di}=1/_{di}).
\(\pi\_{di}\) is the first-order inclusion probability.
\(\pi\_{dij}\) is the joint inclusion probability for units (i) and (j).
\(\Delta*{dij}=\pi{dij}-\pi*{di}\pi{dj}\).

For a binary indicator, (y_{di}=1) means the event is true and (y_{di}=0) means it is false.

The Horvitz-Thmpson estimator needs a domain size and requires the weight scale to be compatible with \(N_d\)

\[
\hat{p}^{HT}_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in s_d} w_i y_i
\]

Meanwhile, the Hajek estimator uses the weighted sample total

\[
\hat{p}^{ratio}_d =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_i y_i}
{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_i}
\]

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Horvitz-Thompson Form
The Horvitz-Thompson (HT) estimator of the domain total is:
\[
\hat{T}^{HT}_d =
\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}y_{di}.
\]
If (N_d) is known, the HT estimator of the domain mean or proportion is:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d =
\frac{\hat{T}^{HT}_d}{N_d}
=
\frac{1}{N_d}\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}y_{di}.
\]
For binary indicators, (^{HT}_d) is the direct estimate of the proportion in domain (d).

title: Direct Estimation
Direct Estimation
text:
4 Working Variance Approximation
When only a final weight is available, use a transparent weighted-residual approximation:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d\right)
\approx
\frac{1}{N_d^2}
\sum_{i \in s_d}
w_{di}^{2}
\left(y_{di}-\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d\right)^2.
\]
The standard error is:
\[
\widehat{SE}\left(\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d\right)
=
\sqrt{\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d\right)}.
\]
The approximation is useful for teaching and first-pass SAE inputs. Replace it with a full design-based variance estimator where survey design information is available.

direct_estimates <- sample_indicator |>
  left_join(domain_sizes, by = "la_code") |>
  group_by(la_code) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    non_missing_n = sum(!is.na(indicator)),
    domain_size = first(domain_size),
    direct = sum(weight * indicator, na.rm = TRUE) / first(domain_size),
    variance = sum(weight^2 * (indicator - direct)^2, na.rm = TRUE) /
      first(domain_size)^2,
    se = sqrt(variance),
    lower = direct - 1.96 * se,
    upper = direct + 1.96 * se,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

title: Direct Estimation
text:
5 Survey-Design Version
Where design variables are available, prefer a survey package design object.

library(survey)

des <- svydesign(
  ids = ~psu,
  strata = ~stratum,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = sample_indicator,
  nest = TRUE
)

direct_total <- svyby(
  ~indicator,
  ~domain_id,
  des,
  svytotal,
  na.rm = TRUE,
  vartype = c("se", "ci")
)

direct <- direct_total |>
  left_join(domain_sizes, by = "domain_id") |>
  mutate(
    direct = indicator / domain_size,
    se_direct = se / domain_size,
    variance = se_direct^2
  )

If the weight scale is uncertain and the target is a mean or proportion, svymean gives a ratio-style estimate closer to the Hájek form.

title: Direct Estimation
section: 6 Scenario 1: Known Domain Size and Suitable Grossing Weight
text:
6 Scenario 1: Known Domain Size and Suitable Grossing Weight
Use this cleanest direct-estimation case when the domain size is known and the selected weight grosses to the same target population.
The HT total is:
\[
\hat{T}^{HT}_d =
\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}y_{di}.
\]
The HT mean or proportion is:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}y_{di}.
\]
The working variance approximation is:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d\right)
\approx
\frac{1}{N_d^2}
\sum_{i \in s_d}
w_{di}^{2}
\left(y_{di}-\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d\right)^2.
\]
Use this route when:

The survey has a domain identifier.
The outcome is observed or explicitly coded for the target population.
The selected weight grosses to the target population.
A domain-size table is available for every target domain.


direct <- calculate_direct_estimates(
  survey_file = "data/clean/ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv",
  domain_file = "data/clean/pop_estimate.csv",
  indicator = "walk_weekly",
  domain_id = "la_code",
  weight = "WalesTravelWeight",
  domain_size = "pop_16plus"
)

title: Direct Estimation
section: 7 Scenario 2: Known Domain Size but Uncertain Weight Scale
text:
7 Scenario 2: Known Domain Size but Uncertain Weight Scale
Some surveys provide weights that are calibrated but not grossed to the population total. If the documentation does not clearly state that the weight scale matches (N_d), a ratio estimator is usually safer for means and proportions.
The Hájek or ratio estimator is:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{H}_d =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}y_{di}}
{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}}.
\]
Define the weighted sample size:
\[
\hat{N}^{w}_d =
\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}.
\]
A working variance approximation is:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right)
\approx
\frac{1}{\left(\hat{N}^{w}_d\right)^2}
\sum_{i \in s_d}
w_{di}^{2}
\left(y_{di}-\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right)^2.
\]
If it is defensible to rescale weights to the known domain size, define:
\[
\tilde{w}_{di}
=
w_{di}
\frac{N_d}{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}}.
\]
Then:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{HT,rescaled}_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in s_d}\tilde{w}_{di}y_{di}
=
\hat{\theta}^{H}_d.
\]
The corresponding working variance is:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{HT,rescaled}_d\right)
\approx
\frac{1}{N_d^2}
\sum_{i \in s_d}
\tilde{w}_{di}^{2}
\left(y_{di}-\hat{\theta}^{HT,rescaled}_d\right)^2.
\]
The ratio form is useful for proportions. Be cautious with totals: a normalised weight does not estimate a population count unless it has been rescaled to a defensible population total.

direct_weighted_mean <- survey_data |>
  group_by(domain_id) |>
  summarise(
    direct = weighted.mean(indicator, weight, na.rm = TRUE),
    weight_sum = sum(weight, na.rm = TRUE),
    non_missing_n = sum(!is.na(indicator)),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

title: Direct Estimation
text:
8 Scenario 3: Unknown Domain Size
Unknown domain sizes are common for routed populations such as commuters, bus users, or train users at fine geography.
If (N_d) is unknown, the HT mean cannot be calculated in the form:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{HT}_d =
\frac{1}{N_d}
\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}y_{di}.
\]
For means and proportions, use the Hájek estimator:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{H}_d =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}y_{di}}
{\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}}.
\]
with working variance:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right)
\approx
\frac{1}{\left(\sum_{i \in s_d}w_{di}\right)^2}
\sum_{i \in s_d}
w_{di}^{2}
\left(y_{di}-\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right)^2.
\]
If a total or count is required, an external or modelled domain size (_d) is needed:
\[
\hat{T}_d =
\tilde{N}_d\hat{\theta}^{H}_d.
\]
If (_d) is treated as fixed:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{T}_d\right)
\approx
\tilde{N}_d^2
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right).
\]
If (_d) is itself estimated, its uncertainty should also be propagated. A simple delta-method approximation is:
\[
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{T}_d\right)
\approx
\tilde{N}_d^2\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right)
+
\left(\hat{\theta}^{H}_d\right)^2
\widehat{\operatorname{Var}}\left(\tilde{N}_d\right)
+ 2\tilde{N}_d\hat{\theta}^{H}_d
\widehat{\operatorname{Cov}}\left(\hat{\theta}^{H}_d,\tilde{N}_d\right).
\]
Possible responses:

Redefine the estimand to use a known denominator, such as all adults.
Estimate the denominator separately using census, administrative, or modelled auxiliary data.
Move to a coarser geography where denominator estimates are available.
Use a unit-level or poststratification approach if population cell counts are available.
Report the limitation explicitly and avoid overstating population interpretation.







Warning



Do not quietly use the all-adult denominator for a routed-group estimand. “Share of train users who are dissatisfied” and “share of adults who are dissatisfied train users” answer different questions.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
9 Diagnostics Before Modelling

Which domains have no sampled observations?
Which domains have very small non-missing sample sizes?
Which estimates are outside a plausible range after uncertainty is considered?
Which domains are dominated by one or two large weights?
Are the estimated sampling variances zero, missing, or implausibly small?

title: Direct Estimation
text:
10 What Next?







Direct-estimate pattern
Next step




Most domains have adequate sample sizes and uncertainty is acceptable
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Some domains are noisy but still have usable direct estimates and variances
Fit a Fay-Herriot model.


Many domains have no sample or unusable variances
Revisit the geography, denominator, covariates, and whether SAE is defensible.


Estimates show strong residual spatial pattern
Consider a spatial extension after fitting a non-spatial baseline.


Estimates depend on uncertain denominators or routed variables
Pause before modelling and resolve the estimand, denominator, and weight logic.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
11 Notes on Sources
The notation here follows standard direct-estimation material for SAE: HT direct estimators use only sample units from the target domain and require a known (N_d) for domain means, while Hájek estimators replace (N_d) with the weighted sample total when the domain size or weight scale is uncertain. See the SAE poverty mapping direct-estimates guide and the SUMMER small-area estimation vignette for related examples.

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
Beginner-friendly Small Area Estimation (SAE) for researchers estimating survey-derived metrics across a whole country or region at fine geographic resolution.
The site has two tracks: a theory track that explains the SAE workflow, and a practical tutorial track that applies the workflow to a dataset. It uses the National Survey for Wales as the first worked survey.
The main practical uses R, Quarto, and SAE packages such as survey, sae, SUMMER, and INLA.Make sure you have R version 4.5.1 installed. You can download it from CRAN.

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
1 Why Small Area Estimation?
[draft] Survey are often not precise enough for fine geographies such as Local Authorities, MSOAs, or LSOAs. Some areas have very few/no respondents, unequal weights Taking weighted meands can be unstable. Also out of domain sample
SAE is useful when direct estimates are too noisy or incomplete. Area-level SAE use direct estimates and their sampling variances, borrow strength across areas using covariates and random effects.
SAE can be further improved with bayesian statistics (to smooth out area) and add spatial structure (spatial autocorrelation)
SAE combines survey data, population estimates, auxiliary data, modelling to estimate the unsurveyed in an informed way.

text:
2 Tutorial Pathway

No.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text: After direct estimation and SAE modelling, check whether the estimates are stable, interpretable, and useful for reporting.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
Area-level SAE models are usually the first model-based step after direct estimation. They are useful when direct estimates exist for at least some domains but are too noisy, unstable, or missing for others.
The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model. It does not model individual survey records. Instead, it models domain-level direct estimates and their sampling variances.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
1 When Fay-Herriot Is Useful
Use an area-level model when:

You have direct estimates for sampled domains.
You have an estimated sampling variance for each direct estimate.
You have domain-level covariates for every domain you want to estimate.
Unit-level population microdata are unavailable, restricted, or not comparable with the survey.
The target output is one estimate per area.

FH is particularly attractive for surveys where the public or project-safe data can be prepared as one row per domain.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
2 Required Inputs
For each domain (d=1,,D), the FH model needs:

(^{DIR}_d): direct estimate from the survey.
(_d): estimated sampling variance of the direct estimate.
(x_d): a vector of area-level covariates.
A domain identifier that aligns across survey, covariate, and boundary files.

The model is usually fitted on domains with usable direct estimates and variances. Predictions can then be produced for unsampled domains using the covariates and model structure.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
3 Two-Stage Model
The FH model is often described as two linked models.

3.1 Sampling Model
The direct estimate is treated as a noisy observation of the true domain value:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d = \theta_d + e_d,
\]
where:
\[
e_d \sim N(0, \psi_d).
\]
Here, (_d) is the sampling variance estimated during direct estimation. The model treats (_d) as known, even though in practice it is estimated.


3.2 Linking Model
The true domain value is related to area-level covariates:
\[
\theta_d = x_d^\top\beta + u_d,
\]
where:
\[
u_d \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2).
\]
The random effect (u_d) captures between-area variation not explained by the covariates.
Combining both pieces gives:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
4 Shrinkage Interpretation
The FH estimate can be written as a weighted average of:

the direct estimate (^{DIR}_d), and
the regression-synthetic estimate (x_d^).

The empirical best linear unbiased predictor (EBLUP) is:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d =
\hat{\gamma}_d\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+
(1-\hat{\gamma}_d)x_d^\top\hat{\beta}.
\]
The shrinkage factor is:
\[
\hat{\gamma}_d =
\frac{\hat{\sigma}_u^2}
{\hat{\sigma}_u^2+\psi_d}.
\]
Interpretation:

If (_d) is small, the direct estimate is precise and (_d) is close to 1.
If (_d) is large, the direct estimate is noisy and (_d) is closer to 0.
Unsampled domains have no direct estimate, so their estimates rely on the synthetic component and any modelled area effect structure.

This is why FH estimates should usually stay close to precise direct estimates but move noisy direct estimates toward the model prediction.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
5 Estimating Model Uncertainty
FH uncertainty is usually summarised through an estimated mean squared error:
\[
\operatorname{MSE}\left(\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d\right)
=
E\left[
\left(
\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d-\theta_d
\right)^2
\right].
\]
In practice, packages such as sae return an MSE estimate for the EBLUP. The square root of the MSE is often reported as an RMSE or model standard error.
Be careful: FH MSE is model-based. It is not the same object as the sampling variance of a direct estimate.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
6 Baseline sae Pattern

library(dplyr)
library(sae)

fh_data <- direct_estimates |>
  left_join(covariates, by = "domain_id")

fh_fit <- eblupFH(
  direct ~ prop_no_car + prop_urban + median_age,
  vardir = variance,
  data = fh_data
)

fh_results <- fh_data |>
  mutate(
    fh_estimate = fh_fit$eblup$eblup,
    fh_mse = fh_fit$mse$mse,
    fh_rmse = sqrt(fh_mse)
  )

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
7 Covariate Choice
Use covariates that are:

available for every target domain,
measured at the correct geography,
plausibly related to the indicator,
comparable in time to the survey,
not too collinear with each other,
interpretable enough to explain to users.

For transport and energy indicators, useful covariate families might include age structure, household composition, car availability, urban-rural classification, housing tenure, employment, deprivation, public transport access, and energy-related housing characteristics.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
8 Practical Checks
Before trusting the model, check:

Are direct estimates and variances plausible?
Are zero or missing sampling variances handled?
Are covariate signs and magnitudes plausible?
Are predictions outside the valid range for proportions?
Do FH estimates stay close to precise direct estimates?
Which areas are strongly pulled toward the model?
Do residuals show remaining spatial pattern?

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
9 Limitations
FH is often practical, but it has important limitations:

It assumes the direct estimate is approximately normal.
It treats estimated sampling variances as known.
It uses one observation per domain, so the number of model observations can be small.
Each indicator usually needs a separate model.
It cannot produce sub-domain estimates unless a new model is built at that geography.
For proportions near 0 or 1, a transformation or alternative model may be needed.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
10 Transformations for Proportions
For binary indicators, plain FH can produce estimates below 0 or above 1. This is most likely when direct estimates are near the boundary or sampling variances are large.
Possible responses:

Use a logit or arcsine transformation.
Fit a binomial or beta-binomial model where appropriate.
Treat boundary estimates and zero variances carefully before modelling.
Report when transformed estimates have been back-transformed.

The right choice depends on the indicator, sample size, and modelling package.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
11 What to Report
At minimum, report:

direct estimate,
direct sampling variance or standard error,
FH estimate,
FH MSE or RMSE,
sample size or non-missing sample size,
covariates used,
areas where model estimates differ strongly from direct estimates.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
12 Notes on Sources
This page follows the usual FH presentation as a sampling model plus linking model, with the EBLUP expressed as a weighted average of the direct and regression-synthetic estimates. See the area-level SAE chapter in the poverty mapping guide for a fuller worked discussion.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text: A survey outcome becomes SAE-ready through a clear estimand, a coded indicator, a target domain, a domain size, and a compatible survey weight. To see this workflow in action, go to Tutorial: Data Preparation.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
1 Defining the Estimand
Before fitting any SAE model, define exactly what the survey estimate should represent. This so-called estimand determines how SAE-ready inputs are prepared and how to choose the most appropriate estimation approach downstream. It is good practice to formulate the estimands of interest in this format:

Estimate the measure of what outcome, among which population, across which geographic domains.

E.g.,

Share of adults who use the bus weekly across MSOA
Mean commute distance among workers across Wards

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
2 Constructing the Indicator
After defining the estimand, identify or create the survey indicator that measures the outcome. Here are some of the most common indicator types:








Indicator type
What to define
Example




Binary
What is 1, 0, missing, and out of scope. Also suitable for categorical variables
Estimand: Share of adults who use the bus weeklyIndicator: Uses bus weekly: yes/no


Continuous
The numeric value and unit
Estimand: Mean commute distance among workers Indicator: Commute distance in miles



Use the survey questionnaire, response codes, and routing rules to guide indicator construction. Some indicators are direct recodes of one survey question; others need to combine several variables. The same raw survey variable can support different estimands.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
3 Domain Size
The domain size \(N_d\) is the size of the target population in each domain \(d\). It is the denominator for domain means and proportions, and is essential for the SAE pipeline.

The target population is the group the estimate is about, such as all adults, households, workers, commuters, or public transport users, etc.
The target domain is the geographic area for which estimates will be produced, such as Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA, etc.







Note



Domain sizes can often be obtained from census datasets or other regional aggregate statistics. If the domain size is unknown for the estimand of interest, alternative direct estimation methods may be used. Otherwise, the estimand may also be refined, e.g., using coarser geography.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
4 Survey Weight

4.1 Why it matters
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent proportions observed in the real world.
The Survey User Guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, how they are derived, and which survey modules/questions they apply to. The weighting methodology commonly includes:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For SAE, the weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. There is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection for a SAE workflow.






Note



If the estimand has no compatible weight variable provided by the survey, alternative direct estimation methods may be used.




4.2 Grossing Weights vs. Analysis Weights
For SAE, it is also important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable.

Some weights are grossing weights \(w^{G}\). They make proportions representative of the target population and sum to a population total or domain size \(N_d\).
Other weights are analysis weights \(w^{A}\). They make proportions representative of the target population, but often sum to the achieved sample size \(n_d\).







Note



The SAE workflow in this tutorial prefers the setup where grossing weight and domain size are available, because it gives a direct link between the survey respondent records and the population count in each domain.
If only analysis weights are provided and

the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula \(w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}\)
the domain sies are unknown, alternative direct estimation methods may be used.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
5 Preparing SAE-ready dataset
At the end of this stage, each indicator should have a short record that defines what will be passed into further SAE steps.







Item
What to prepare




Estimand
The outcome, target population, domain, and time period


Indicator
The coded survey variable used for estimation


Indicator construction
Source variable(s), response codes, routing rules, and rules for 1, 0, missing, and out-of-scope cases


Target domain
The geography for reporting


Domain size
The denominator \(N_d\) for each domain (or n/a)


Weight variable
The survey weight \(w_i\) (or n/a)


Weight scale
Whether the weight is sample-scaled or grossing-scaled


Direct estimator
The estimator implied by the domain size and weight scale, such as HT or Hajek/ratio


Other Survey Design information
Any strata, clusters, replicate weights, or routing variables needed for uncertainty



The goal is to produce one analysis-ready table per indicator, with one row per survey respondent and the variables needed for direct estimation, plus one domain-size table with one row per target domain. The respondent-level table should contain at least the domain ID, indicator, selected weight, and any design or routing variables needed for uncertainty estimation.

title: Bayesian Area and Spatial Smoothing
text: Spatial SAE adds structure when neighbouring domains are expected to have similar values after accounting for covariates. Bayesian SAE extends this by representing uncertainty through posterior distributions.

title: Bayesian Area and Spatial Smoothing
text:
1 When spatial structure helps
Spatial modelling is most defensible when:

The indicator has a plausible spatial process.
Domains are geographically meaningful for the process.
Residuals from a non-spatial model show spatial patterning.
The adjacency structure is reliable and aligned with the modelling table.

title: Bayesian Area and Spatial Smoothing
text:
2 When to be cautious
Spatial smoothing can hide real local differences. It can also create overconfident maps if adjacency is treated as a substitute for missing survey signal.

title: Bayesian Area and Spatial Smoothing
text:
3 Adjacency checks

library(sf)
library(spdep)

domains_sf <- st_read("../data/boundaries/boundaries-ltla.geojson")

neighbors <- poly2nb(domains_sf, queen = TRUE)
adjacency <- nb2mat(neighbors, style = "B", zero.policy = TRUE)

stopifnot(nrow(adjacency) == nrow(domains_sf))

title: Bayesian Area and Spatial Smoothing
text:
4 Bayesian FH with spatial effects
This section should introduce the practical pattern for SUMMER and INLA:

Prepare direct estimates and variances.
Prepare domain covariates.
Prepare an adjacency graph.
Fit a non-spatial Bayesian FH model.
Fit a spatial FH model.
Compare posterior summaries, residuals, and mapped uncertainty.

title: Bayesian Area and Spatial Smoothing
text:
5 Outputs to communicate

Posterior mean or median.
Credible intervals.
Exceedance probabilities for policy thresholds.
Maps of uncertainty, not only maps of point estimates.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
text:
3 Domain Size and Survey Weights

3.1 Choosing domain size
The domain size \(N_d\) is the size of the target population in each domain \(d\). It is the denominator for domain means and proportions, and is essential for the SAE pipeline.

The target population is the group the estimate is about, such as all adults, households, workers, commuters, or public transport users, etc.
The target domain is the geographic area for which estimates will be produced, such as Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA, etc.

Domain sizes can often be obtained from census datasets or other regional aggregate statistics.


3.2 Why survey weight matters
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent proportions observed in the real world.
The Survey User Guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, how they are derived, and which survey modules/questions they apply to. The weighting methodology commonly includes:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For SAE, the weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. There is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection for a SAE workflow.


3.3 Grossing Weights vs. Analysis Weights
For SAE, it is also important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable, as it implies different estimation approaches.

Some weights are grossing weights \(w^{G}\). They make proportions representative of the target population and sum to a population total or domain size \(N_d\).
Other weights are analysis weights \(w^{A}\). They make proportions representative of the target population, but often sum to the achieved sample size \(n_d\).

If analysis weights are provided and the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula \(w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}\) if required.






Note



The SAE workflow in this tutorial prefers the setup where grossing weights are provided for each survey respondent record and domain sizes are available, because it gives a direct link between the survey respondent records and the population count in each domain.
If the domain size is unknown and/or no compatible weight variables are provided for the estimand of interest, alternative direct estimation methods may be used. Otherwise, the estimand may also be redefined.

title: From Estimands to SAE Inputs
section: 3 Choosing Domain Sizes and Survey Weights
text:
3 Choosing Domain Sizes and Survey Weights

3.1 Domain size
The domain size \(N_d\) is the size of the target population in each domain \(d\). It is the denominator for domain means and proportions, and is essential for the SAE pipeline. Domain sizes can often be obtained from census datasets or other regional aggregate statistics.

The target population is the group the estimate is about, such as all adults, households, workers, commuters, or public transport users, etc.
The target domain is the geographic area for which estimates will be produced, such as Local Authority, Ward, LSOA, or MSOA, etc.

The choice of target domain, besides being shaped by the estimand definition, should also be informed by data availability. For example,

if survey respondent records are only identified at the Local Authority level and no finer, then Local Authority is the most granular target domain for SAE
if the domain sizes are only available at the Ward level and no finer (e.g., total worker population by Ward), then it is recommended to use Ward as the most granular target domain for SAE

[ADD IMAGE]


3.2 Survey weight
Survey weights connect the sample back to the population to ensure the survey sample can best represent real-world proportions. The Survey User Guide is the go-to resource to understand which weight variables are available, how they are derived, and which survey modules/questions they apply to. In general, weight variables are derived to adjust for one or more aspects, such as:

Selection adjustment: accounts for unequal probabilities of selection.
Non-response adjustment: compensates for differences between respondents and non-respondents.
Calibration adjustment: aligns the weighted sample with known population totals, such as age, sex, region, Local Authority, tenure, or household structure.
Subsample adjustment: accounts for cases where only part of the sample was asked a module or completed a diary.

For a SAE workflow, there is no one-size-fits-all approach to survey weight selection. The weight used must match the estimand unit, target population, and survey design. Moreover, it is also important to distinguish the scale of the weight variable, as it implies different estimation approaches.

Some weights are grossing weights \(w^{G}\). They make proportions representative of the target population and sum to a population total or domain size \(N_d\).
Other weights are analysis weights \(w^{A}\). They make proportions representative of the target population, but often sum to the achieved sample size \(n_d\).

If analysis weights are provided and the domain sizes \(N_d\) are known, analysis weights can be scaled to grossing weights for each survey respondent using the following formula \(w^G_{di} = w^A_{di} * \frac{N_d}{\sum w^A_d}\) if required.
[ADD IMAGE]






Note



For the SAE workflow, the preferred setup is one where grossing weights are provided for each survey respondent record and domain sizes are available, because it gives a direct link between the survey respondent records and the population count in each domain.
If the domain size is unknown and/or no compatible weight variables are provided for the estimand of interest, alternative direct estimation methods may be used. Otherwise, the estimand may also be redefined (e.g., coarser geography, less specific target population).

title: Estimation and Diagnostics
text:
The estimation workflow starts from the prepared SAE inputs from the National Survey for Wales example. It will cover:

calculating direct estimates and sampling variances,
checking sample sizes and unstable domains,
fitting area-level SAE models,
comparing direct and modelled estimates,
diagnosing uncertainty and residual patterns,
preparing outputs for reporting.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
1 Model Diagnostics

Compare direct and model-based estimates.
Inspect residuals and remaining spatial pattern.
Review sensitivity to covariates and modelling choices.
Identify domains where the model is driven strongly by covariates rather than local survey information.
Check whether estimates should aggregate to known national or regional totals.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
2 Uncertainty

Decide which uncertainty measure to report: standard error, confidence interval, credible interval, MSE, or relative standard error.
Compare uncertainty before and after modelling.
Flag domains with high uncertainty or very small effective sample sizes.
Map uncertainty as well as point estimates.
Avoid presenting smoothed estimates as if they were observed survey results.

title: Diagnostics, Validation, and Uncertainty
text:
3 Reporting Checks

Use wording that matches the estimand, target population, and domain size.
Report caveats for routed questions, uncertain denominators, or missing domain sizes.
Keep direct estimates available as a baseline comparison.
Document any model choices that materially affect the estimates.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
Area-level SAE models are usually the first model-based step after direct estimation. They are useful when direct estimates exist for at least some domains but are too noisy, unstable, or missing for others.
The standard starting point is the Fay-Herriot (FH) model. It does not model individual survey records. Instead, it models domain-level direct estimates and their sampling variances.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
1 When Fay-Herriot Is Useful
Use an area-level model when:

You have direct estimates for sampled domains.
You have an estimated sampling variance for each direct estimate.
You have domain-level covariates for every domain you want to estimate.
Unit-level population microdata are unavailable, restricted, or not comparable with the survey.
The target output is one estimate per area.

FH is particularly attractive for surveys where the public or project-safe data can be prepared as one row per domain.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
2 Required Inputs
For each domain (d=1,,D), the FH model needs:

(^{DIR}_d): direct estimate from the survey.
(_d): estimated sampling variance of the direct estimate.
(x_d): a vector of area-level covariates.
A domain identifier that aligns across survey, covariate, and boundary files.

The model is usually fitted on domains with usable direct estimates and variances. Predictions can then be produced for unsampled domains using the covariates and model structure.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
3 Two-Stage Model
The FH model is often described as two linked models.

3.1 Sampling Model
The direct estimate is treated as a noisy observation of the true domain value:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d = \theta_d + e_d,
\]
where:
\[
e_d \sim N(0, \psi_d).
\]
Here, (_d) is the sampling variance estimated during direct estimation. The model treats (_d) as known, even though in practice it is estimated.


3.2 Linking Model
The true domain value is related to area-level covariates:
\[
\theta_d = x_d^\top\beta + u_d,
\]
where:
\[
u_d \sim N(0, \sigma_u^2).
\]
The random effect (u_d) captures between-area variation not explained by the covariates.
Combining both pieces gives:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d =
x_d^\top\beta + u_d + e_d.
\]

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
4 Shrinkage Interpretation
The FH estimate can be written as a weighted average of:

the direct estimate (^{DIR}_d), and
the regression-synthetic estimate (x_d^).

The empirical best linear unbiased predictor (EBLUP) is:
\[
\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d =
\hat{\gamma}_d\hat{\theta}^{DIR}_d
+
(1-\hat{\gamma}_d)x_d^\top\hat{\beta}.
\]
The shrinkage factor is:
\[
\hat{\gamma}_d =
\frac{\hat{\sigma}_u^2}
{\hat{\sigma}_u^2+\psi_d}.
\]
Interpretation:

If (_d) is small, the direct estimate is precise and (_d) is close to 1.
If (_d) is large, the direct estimate is noisy and (_d) is closer to 0.
Unsampled domains have no direct estimate, so their estimates rely on the synthetic component and any modelled area effect structure.

This is why FH estimates should usually stay close to precise direct estimates but move noisy direct estimates toward the model prediction.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
5 Estimating Model Uncertainty
FH uncertainty is usually summarised through an estimated mean squared error:
\[
\operatorname{MSE}\left(\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d\right)
=
E\left[
\left(
\hat{\theta}^{FH}_d-\theta_d
\right)^2
\right].
\]
In practice, packages such as sae return an MSE estimate for the EBLUP. The square root of the MSE is often reported as an RMSE or model standard error.
Be careful: FH MSE is model-based. It is not the same object as the sampling variance of a direct estimate.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
6 Baseline sae Pattern

library(dplyr)
library(sae)

fh_data <- direct_estimates |>
  left_join(covariates, by = "domain_id")

fh_fit <- eblupFH(
  direct ~ prop_urban + median_age + employment_rate,
  vardir = variance,
  data = fh_data
)

fh_results <- fh_data |>
  mutate(
    fh_estimate = fh_fit$eblup$eblup,
    fh_mse = fh_fit$mse$mse,
    fh_rmse = sqrt(fh_mse)
  )

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
7 Covariate Choice
Use covariates that are:

available for every target domain,
measured at the correct geography,
plausibly related to the indicator,
comparable in time to the survey,
not too collinear with each other,
interpretable enough to explain to users.

For transport and energy indicators, useful covariate families might include age structure, household composition, car availability, urban-rural classification, housing tenure, employment, deprivation, public transport access, and energy-related housing characteristics.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
8 Practical Checks
Before trusting the model, check:

Are direct estimates and variances plausible?
Are zero or missing sampling variances handled?
Are covariate signs and magnitudes plausible?
Are predictions outside the valid range for proportions?
Do FH estimates stay close to precise direct estimates?
Which areas are strongly pulled toward the model?
Do residuals show remaining spatial pattern?

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
9 Limitations
FH is often practical, but it has important limitations:

It assumes the direct estimate is approximately normal.
It treats estimated sampling variances as known.
It uses one observation per domain, so the number of model observations can be small.
Each indicator usually needs a separate model.
It cannot produce sub-domain estimates unless a new model is built at that geography.
For proportions near 0 or 1, a transformation or alternative model may be needed.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
10 Transformations for Proportions
For binary indicators, plain FH can produce estimates below 0 or above 1. This is most likely when direct estimates are near the boundary or sampling variances are large.
Possible responses:

Use a logit or arcsine transformation.
Fit a binomial or beta-binomial model where appropriate.
Treat boundary estimates and zero variances carefully before modelling.
Report when transformed estimates have been back-transformed.

The right choice depends on the indicator, sample size, and modelling package.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
11 What to Report
At minimum, report:

direct estimate,
direct sampling variance or standard error,
FH estimate,
FH MSE or RMSE,
sample size or non-missing sample size,
covariates used,
areas where model estimates differ strongly from direct estimates.

title: Area-Level SAE Models
text:
12 Notes on Sources
The FH presentation above follows the usual sampling-model plus linking-model structure, with the EBLUP expressed as a weighted average of the direct and regression-synthetic estimates. See the area-level SAE chapter in the poverty mapping guide for a fuller worked discussion.

title: Data Preparation
text: The National Survey for Wales (NSW) 2022-23 respondent file provides a concrete example of the input-preparation workflow from From Estimands to SAE Inputs.

title: Data Preparation
text:
1 Indicator Families
The NSW preparation workflow includes indicators from several families:

household car access,
commuting mode and commuting distance,
hybrid and remote working,
bus and train use,
bus and train satisfaction,
walking and cycling frequency.

The examples below focus on three indicators that illustrate common estimand choices.

title: Data Preparation
text:
2 Example 1: Households Without Car Access
Notebook indicator:

no_car = 1 when CarUse indicates no access to a car.

Estimand:

Estimate the share of households without access to a car in each Local Authority.








Component
NSW example




Outcome
Household has no access to a car


Target population
All households


Target domain
Local Authority


Domain size
Number of households in each Local Authority, household_total


Weight
Household grossing weight, scaled from SampleHhWeight


Interpretation
“x% of households in this Local Authority do not have access to a car.”



This is a household estimand, so the denominator should be households, not adults. The downstream direct estimate should therefore use a household weight and a household domain size.

title: Data Preparation
text:
3 Example 2: Car Commuting
Notebook indicator:

commute_car = 1 when TravWkModeAll4 = 1 or TravWkModeLong = 4.

This survey question is routed through work and commuting questions, so the target population must be chosen carefully.









Possible estimand
Target population
Domain size
Interpretation




Share of adults who commute by car
All adults aged 16+
Adult population, pop_16plus
“x% of adults in this Local Authority are car commuters.”


Share of commuters who commute by car
Commuters
Number of commuters
“x% of commuters in this Local Authority commute by car.”



The NSW notebook uses the first version for the current workflow: all adults aged 16+ as the target population, pop_16plus as the domain size, and WalesAdultWeight as the weight. That makes the indicator an adult-population prevalence measure, not a proportion among commuters only.
If the aim changes to estimating the share of commuters who use a car, the domain size would need to change to the number of commuters in each Local Authority. If that denominator is not available, the direct estimation method or spatial resolution may need to change.

title: Data Preparation
text:
4 Example 3: Regular Bus Use
Notebook indicator:

pt_bus_use = 1 when BusFreq12M = 1, meaning bus use at least once a week.

Estimand:

Estimate the share of adults who use the bus at least weekly in each Local Authority.








Component
NSW example




Outcome
Uses the bus at least weekly


Target population
All adults aged 16+


Target domain
Local Authority


Domain size
Adult population in each Local Authority, pop_16plus


Weight
WalesAdultWeight


Interpretation
“x% of adults in this Local Authority use the bus at least weekly.”



This is different from estimating the share of bus users who use the bus weekly. In the current definition, non-users are part of the adult target population. If the estimand were restricted to public transport users, the denominator and possibly the weight would need to change.

5 Summary

The same process should be repeated for every indicator before calculating direct estimates: define the outcome, target population, domain, domain size, weight, and interpretation.

title: Data Preparation
Data Preparation
text:
1 R Setup

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

# Allows chunks to work when run from the project root or from pages/.
project_root <- if (dir.exists("pages")) "." else ".."

data_root <- file.path(project_root, "data")
raw_dir <- file.path(data_root, "raw")
census_root <- file.path(raw_dir, "census")
clean_dir <- file.path(data_root, "clean")
dir.create(clean_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

respondent_file <- file.path(
  raw_dir,
  "nsw_2022-23_respondent_file_for_ukds.tab"
)

The NSW materials used here are:

the UK Data Service respondent file in data/raw;
the data user guide, for survey design and weight choices;
the telephone questionnaire, for question wording, routing, and response codes;
the variable catalogue, for variable labels and missing-value conventions;
Census 2021 Local Authority age table TS007, used for adult domain sizes.

The routed travel-to-work questions need extra care. The table above follows the notebook and treats them as adult-population prevalence indicators. title: Data Preparation
text:
5 Construct the Indicators
The questionnaire and variable catalogue drive the coding rules:

CarUse = 2 means no car or van is normally available to the household.
TravWkModeAll4 = 1 or TravWkModeLong = 4 indicates car or van use for commuting.
TravWkDist >= 15 defines a long commute in this worked example.
BusFreq12M = 1 and TrainFreq12M = 1 mean at least weekly use in the last 12 months.
BusOverSat and TrainOverSat values 4 or 5 mean fairly or very dissatisfied.
AtFrqWlk10 and AtFrqBke values 1, 2, or 3 mean at least weekly active travel.


mode_cols <- paste0("TravWkModeAll", 1:8)

nsw_with_modes <- nsw |>
  mutate(
    commute_mode_count = rowSums(
      across(all_of(mode_cols), \(x) x == 1),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    commute_mode_all_missing = if_all(all_of(mode_cols), is_missing_code)
  )

indicators <- nsw_with_modes |>
  transmute(
    CaseNo,
    la_code,
    no_car = make_binary(CarUse, yes_values = 2),
    commute_car = case_when(
      is_missing_code(TravWkModeAll4) & is_missing_code(TravWkModeLong) ~ NA_integer_,
      TravWkModeAll4 == 1 | TravWkModeLong == 4 ~ 1L,
      TravWkModeAll4 == 0 & (is_missing_code(TravWkModeLong) | TravWkModeLong != 4) ~ 0L,
      TRUE ~ NA_integer_
    ),
    commute_long = case_when(
      is_missing_code(TravWkDist) ~ NA_integer_,
      TravWkDist >= 15 ~ 1L,
      TRUE ~ 0L
    ),
    commute_multimodal = case_when(
      commute_mode_all_missing ~ NA_integer_,
      commute_mode_count >= 2 ~ 1L,
      TRUE ~ 0L
    ),
    hybrid_remote = case_when(
      is_missing_code(RemWrkHrs) ~ NA_integer_,
      RemWrkHrs > 5 ~ 1L,
      TRUE ~ 0L
    ),
    pt_bus_use = make_binary(BusFreq12M, yes_values = 1),
    pt_train_use = make_binary(TrainFreq12M, yes_values = 1),
    poor_bus_satisfaction = make_binary(BusOverSat, yes_values = c(4, 5)),
    poor_train_satisfaction = make_binary(TrainOverSat, yes_values = c(4, 5)),
    walk_weekly = make_binary(AtFrqWlk10, yes_values = c(1, 2, 3)),
    bike_weekly = make_binary(AtFrqBke, yes_values = c(1, 2, 3))
  )

indicator_names <- setdiff(names(indicators), c("CaseNo", "la_code"))

indicator_missingness <- indicators |>
  summarise(across(all_of(indicator_names), list(
    non_missing = \(x) sum(!is.na(x)),
    missing = \(x) sum(is.na(x))
  ))) |>
  pivot_longer(
    everything(),
    names_to = c("indicator", ".value"),
    names_pattern = "(.+)_(non_missing|missing)$"
  )

indicator_missingness

This missingness check is part of the workflow. A high missing count might be correct for a routed module, but it should not be ignored. It tells us whether the estimand is really about all adults, a subpopulation, or a module-specific respondent group.

title: Data Preparation
Data Preparation
text:
7 Choose and Scale Weights
The NSW guide distinguishes between weights for people, households, and modules. The variable catalogue labels WalesAdultWeight as representing all adults aged 16+ in Wales, while SampleHhWeight and SampleTravelWeight are sample-scale weights. For the direct-estimation workflow in this tutorial, we want weights that are compatible with the domain-size denominator, so the notebook scales the household and travel sample weights to external totals.

wales_households <- sum(domain_sizes$household_total, na.rm = TRUE)
wales_adults <- sum(domain_sizes$pop_16plus, na.rm = TRUE)

weights <- nsw |>
  transmute(
    CaseNo,
    WalesAdultWeight,
    WalesHhWeight =
      SampleHhWeight * wales_households / sum(SampleHhWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    WalesTravelWeight =
      SampleTravelWeight * wales_adults / sum(SampleTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE)
  )

weights |>
  summarise(
    WalesAdultWeight_total = sum(WalesAdultWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    WalesHhWeight_total = sum(WalesHhWeight, na.rm = TRUE),
    WalesTravelWeight_total = sum(WalesTravelWeight, na.rm = TRUE)
  )







Warning



Scaling weights is an analysis choice, not a mechanical step. It is appropriate here because the next page demonstrates direct estimates using a known domain size. If you use a ratio estimator instead, or if official grossing weights are supplied for the exact target population, the preparation step should change.

title: Data Preparation
text:
8 Create SAE Input Files
The direct estimation page expects a respondent-level file with:

CaseNo,
la_code,
one binary indicator,
one compatible weight.

The register below stores the indicator-weight-domain-size choices. It is the practical version of the estimand table.

indicator_register <- tibble::tribble(
  ~indicator, ~weight, ~domain_size,
  "no_car", "WalesHhWeight", "household_total",
  "commute_car", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "commute_long", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "commute_multimodal", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "hybrid_remote", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "pt_bus_use", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "pt_train_use", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "poor_bus_satisfaction", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "poor_train_satisfaction", "WalesAdultWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "walk_weekly", "WalesTravelWeight", "pop_16plus",
  "bike_weekly", "WalesTravelWeight", "pop_16plus"
)

analysis_data <- indicators |>
  left_join(weights, by = "CaseNo")

for (i in seq_len(nrow(indicator_register))) {
  spec <- indicator_register[i, ]

  indicator_file <- analysis_data |>
    select(
      CaseNo,
      la_code,
      all_of(spec$indicator),
      all_of(spec$weight)
    )

  write_csv(
    indicator_file,
    file.path(clean_dir, str_c("ind_weight_", spec$indicator, ".csv"))
  )
}

A quick check should confirm that every output has a domain identifier, an indicator, and the intended weight.

read_csv(
  file.path(clean_dir, "ind_weight_pt_bus_use.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  glimpse()

read_csv(
  file.path(clean_dir, "pop_estimate.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  glimpse()

title: Data Preparation
Data Preparation
text:
1 Getting the Data
A reproducible workflow needs a predictable way to obtain the raw survey file. Three practical approaches are useful.








Approach
How it works
When to use




Project-hosted file
Put the file under data/raw/ and link to it from the notes.
Easiest for workshops and local teaching, if redistribution is allowed.


Download helper
Provide an R function, such as get_nsw_data(), that downloads from a supplied URL if the file is missing.
Best for public materials where the dataset is hosted somewhere stable.


Package data
Build an R package and expose a small processed dataset through data().
Best for small examples, not ideal for a large raw survey file.



The data/ directory is included as a Quarto resource, so the rendered website can provide direct download links:

Download the NSW respondent file
Download Census age table TS007
Download Census household table TS041

A small helper function can provide the same behaviour from R. Replace dataset_url with the public or institutional URL used for the course materials.

get_nsw_data <- function(
  dataset_url,
  dest_dir = NULL,
  file_name = "nsw_2022-23_respondent_file_for_ukds.tab"
) {
  if (is.null(dest_dir)) {
    project_root <- if (dir.exists("pages")) "." else ".."
    dest_dir <- file.path(project_root, "data", "raw")
  }

  dir.create(dest_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)
  dest_file <- file.path(dest_dir, file_name)

  if (!file.exists(dest_file)) {
    download.file(dataset_url, dest_file, mode = "wb")
  }

  dest_file
}







Note



The base R data() pattern usually means packaging a small .rda object inside an R package. That works well for tiny teaching datasets. For a large raw UKDS tab file, a download helper is clearer and keeps the source format visible.

title: Data Preparation

3 Indicator
The indicator is the coded survey variable that measures the outcome in the estimand. The three selected indicators use different parts of the NSW questionnaire.








Indicator
Source variables
Question area




commute_multimodal
TravWkModeAll1 to TravWkModeAll8
Usual modes of travel to work

Overall satisfaction with bus services


walk_weekly
AtFrqWlk10
Walking for transport in the last three months



The routed travel-to-work item needs particular care. In this version, commute_multimodal is treated as an adult-population prevalence measure: the share of all adults who usually use two or more commute modes. A different estimand, such as the share of commuters who are multimodal, would need a commuter domain size.

title: Data Preparation
text:
3 Indicator Construction
The indicator is the coded survey variable that measures the outcome in the estimand. The questionnaire and variable catalogue determine the source variables, routing, and response codes.








Indicator
Source variables
Construction rule




commute_multimodal
TravWkModeAll1 to TravWkModeAll8
1 when two or more commute modes are selected


poor_bus_satisfaction
BusOverSat
1 when BusOverSat is 4 or 5, meaning fairly or very dissatisfied


walk_weekly

Missing and routed values are set to NA, not 0.

mode_cols <- paste0("TravWkModeAll", 1:8)

nsw_with_modes <- nsw_raw |>
  mutate(
    commute_mode_count = rowSums(
      across(all_of(mode_cols), \(x) x == 1),
      na.rm = TRUE
    ),
    commute_mode_all_missing = if_all(all_of(mode_cols), is_missing_code)
  )

indicators <- nsw_with_modes |>
  transmute(
    unit_id = CaseNo,
    commute_multimodal = case_when(
      commute_mode_all_missing ~ NA_integer_,
      commute_mode_count >= 2 ~ 1L,
      TRUE ~ 0L
    ),
    poor_bus_satisfaction = make_binary(BusOverSat, yes_values = c(4, 5)),
    walk_weekly = make_binary(AtFrqWlk10, yes_values = c(1, 2, 3))
  )

title: Data Preparation
text:
4 Target Domain
The target domain is the geography for which estimates will be produced. The NSW user guide describes results at Local Authority level, and the public respondent file contains DvLA but not a finer public geography.
Use Local Authority as the target domain.

survey_domains <- nsw_raw |>
  transmute(
    unit_id = CaseNo,
    domain_id = str_c("W0", as.integer(DvLA) + 6000000L),
    domain_id = recode(
      domain_id,
      "W06000007" = "W06000023", # Powys
      "W06000017" = "W06000024"  # Merthyr Tydfil

title: Data Preparation
text:
5 Domain Size
The domain size must match the target population in the estimand. All three selected estimands use adults aged 16+ as the target population, so the required domain_size is the adult population by Local Authority.
The Census 2021 TS007 age table provides the adult population denominator.

domain_sizes <- read_csv(
  file.path(census_root, "census2021-ts007-ltla.csv"),
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(str_starts(`geography code`, "W")) |>
  transmute(
    domain_id = `geography code`,
    domain_name = geography,
    domain_size =
      `Age: Total; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 4 years and under; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 5 to 9 years; measures: Value` -
      `Age: Aged 10 to 15 years; measures: Value`

commute_multimodal uses WalesAdultWeight;

  transmute(

  summarise(

      CaseNo,
      la_code,
      all_of(spec$indicator),
      all_of(spec$weight)
    )

  write_csv(
    indicator_file,
    file.path(clean_dir, str_c("ind_weight_", spec$indicator, ".csv"))
  )
}

1 Setup

library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(stringr)
library(tidyr)

# Allows chunks to work when run from the project root or from pages/.
project_root <- if (dir.exists("pages")) "." else ".."

data_root <- file.path(project_root, "data")
raw_dir <- file.path(data_root, "raw")
census_root <- file.path(raw_dir, "census")
clean_dir <- file.path(data_root, "clean")
dir.create(clean_dir, recursive = TRUE, showWarnings = FALSE)

respondent_file <- file.path(
  raw_dir,
  "nsw_2022-23_respondent_file_for_ukds.tab"
)

The NSW materials used here are:

the main respondent file in data/raw;
the data user guide and variable catalogue for survey design and weight choices, as well as variable choices
Census 2021 Local Authority tables in data/raw/census

The respondent file is tab-separated. The catalogue and questionnaire use negative values such as -99, -98, -9, -8, and sometimes -7 for refused, don’t know, routed, or other non-substantive responses. Keep those visible at import, then treat them explicitly during indicator construction.

nsw_raw <- read_tsv(respondent_file, show_col_types = FALSE)

missing_codes <- c(-99, -98, -9, -8, -7)

is_missing_code <- function(x) {
  is.na(x) | x %in% missing_codes
}

make_binary <- function(x, yes_values) {
  case_when(
    is_missing_code(x) ~ NA_integer_,
    x %in% yes_values ~ 1L,
    TRUE ~ 0L
  )
}

title: An Urban Researcher’s Guide to Small Area Estimation
text:
4 Resources
Supporting materials for adapting the workflow:

SAE Toolkit collects reusable input formats and helper scripts.

title: SAE Toolkit Lite
text:
1 Minimum inputs and recommended schema
At minimum, an SAE workflow for Urban Analytics needs:

a survey indicator file, with one row per sampled unit used in the estimate
a domain-size file, with one row per target domain
a covariate file, when fitting an area-level model
boundary geometry to map results or drive spatial structure for spatially-informed models


1.1 Survey indicator file
A survey indicator file links each sampled unit to the chosen outcome, target domain, and survey weight. Survey documentation should be kept alongside the file so that routing, missingness, design variables, and weight choice can be checked.



Column
Meaning




unit_id
Survey respondent, household, or sampled unit ID.


domain_id
Reporting domain code.


indicator
Numeric outcome, or binary 0/1.


weight
Survey weight selected for the estimand.


stratum
(optional) survey stratum.





1.2 Domain-size file
A domain-size file gives the denominator for each target domain. The denominator must match the estimand’s target population rather than the full resident population by default.



Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


domain_size
Denominator matching the target population.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.





1.3 Geometry file
Boundary geometry is useful for visualising estimates and for deriving adjacency structures for spatial models. It is not required for direct estimates or non-spatial area-level models.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code, matching the domain-size file.


domain_name
Human-readable area name.


geometry
Polygon or multipolygon geometry for the reporting domain.





1.4 Covariate file for area-level estimation
Covariates are area-level predictors available for every target domain. They should be measured on the same geography as the direct estimates, or aggregated to that geography before modelling.







Column
Meaning




domain_id
Reporting domain code.


covariate_*
Area-level predictor available for every target domain.


median_age
Example demographic covariate.


prop_urban
Example settlement or land-use covariate.


employment_rate
Example socioeconomic covariate.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
1 Inputs
The direct-estimation step uses the standard files prepared earlier:







File
Required fields




Survey indicator file
unit_id, domain_id, indicator, weight


Domain-size file
domain_id, domain_size



The indicator column can be binary, such as 1 for adults who walk weekly and 0 otherwise, or numeric for a continuous outcome.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
2 Choosing the Direct Estimator
The estimator should match the information available in the survey file and the estimand definition.









Situation
Estimator
Strengths
Main caution




Domain size is known and grossing weight available
Horvitz-Thompson estimator
Direct link between weighted survey totals and population denominator
Weight scale must match the domain-size definition


Domain size is unknown and/or only analysis weight available
Hájek estimator
Robust for means and proportions when weight scale is uncertain
Does not estimate totals




2.1 Horvitz-Thompson Estimator With Known Domain Size
For means and proportions in domain (\(d\)), the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d\) is calculated as:
\[
\hat{\theta}_d =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di} y_{di}}{N_d}
\]
where \(N_d\) is domain_size, \(w_{di}\) is weight, and \(y_{di}\) is indicator. The variance approximation for this direct estimates is: \[
\widehat{V}(\hat{\theta}_d)
\approx
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}^{2}(y_{di} - \hat{\theta}_d)^2}{N_d^2}.
\]


2.2 Hájek Estimator With Unknown Domain Size
For means and proportions in domain (\(d\)), the direct estimate \(\hat{\theta}_d\) is calculated as:
\[
\hat{\theta}_d =
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di} y_{di}}
{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}}.
\]
where \(w_{di}\) is weight, and \(y_{di}\) is indicator. The variance approximation for this direct estimates is:
\[
\widehat{V}(\hat{\theta}_d)
\approx
\frac{\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}^{2}(y_{di} - \hat{\theta}_d)^2}
{\left(\sum_{i \in s_d} w_{di}\right)^2}.
\]






Note



So far in our variance estimation, we assume the survey has no stratification information and is conducted without replacement (i.e., no duplicate respondents). The good news is that the R package sae handles the choice of estimator HT vs. Hájek and survey design features (e.g., stratification and with/without replacement) automatically when performing direct estimation with sae::direct().

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Horvitz-Thompson With Known Domain Size
For a proportion or mean in domain (d):
[ _d = ]
where (N_d) is domain_size, (w_{di}) is weight, and (y_{di}) is indicator.
When full design variance information is unavailable, a transparent first-pass variance approximation is:
[ (_d) . ]

library(dplyr)
library(readr)

survey_indicator <- read_csv(
  "data/clean/ind_weight_walk_weekly.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

domain_sizes <- read_csv(
  "data/clean/pop_estimate.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
)

direct_estimates <- survey_indicator |>
  left_join(domain_sizes, by = "domain_id") |>
  group_by(domain_id) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    non_missing_n = sum(!is.na(indicator)),
    domain_size = first(domain_size),
    direct = sum(weight * indicator, na.rm = TRUE) / domain_size,
    variance = sum(weight^2 * (indicator - direct)^2, na.rm = TRUE) /
      domain_size^2,
    se = sqrt(variance),
    lower = direct - 1.96 * se,
    upper = direct + 1.96 * se,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

direct_estimates

This creates the core area-level input for the next stage: one estimate and one sampling variance per domain.

title: Direct Estimation
section: 4 Ratio Estimator When Weight Scale Is Uncertain
text:
4 Ratio Estimator When Weight Scale Is Uncertain
For means and proportions, the ratio estimator replaces the external denominator with the weighted sample total:
[ d = {{i s_d} w_{di}}. ]

ratio_direct_estimates <- survey_indicator |>
  group_by(domain_id) |>
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    non_missing_n = sum(!is.na(indicator)),
    weight_sum = sum(weight[!is.na(indicator)], na.rm = TRUE),
    direct = weighted.mean(indicator, weight, na.rm = TRUE),
    variance = sum(weight^2 * (indicator - direct)^2, na.rm = TRUE) /
      weight_sum^2,
    se = sqrt(variance),
    lower = direct - 1.96 * se,
    upper = direct + 1.96 * se,
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ratio_direct_estimates

Use this for proportions when the survey documentation supports the weight for analysis, but does not show that it grosses to the domain population. Do not use it for population totals without an external denominator.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Using Survey Design Information
If the survey provides strata, clusters, or replicate weights, use them for the variance estimate. The exact setup depends on the survey documentation.

library(survey)

des <- svydesign(
  ids = ~psu,
  strata = ~stratum,
  weights = ~weight,
  data = survey_indicator,
  nest = TRUE
)

direct_design <- svyby(
  ~indicator,
  ~domain_id,
  des,
  svymean,
  na.rm = TRUE,
  vartype = c("se", "ci")
)

The design-based version is preferable when the required design variables are available. For public-use files, they may be absent or simplified.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Checks Before Modelling
Before fitting an SAE model, check:

domains with no sampled units
domains with very small non_missing_n
zero, missing, or implausibly small variance
estimates outside the valid range for the indicator
domains dominated by one or two large weights
mismatch between the weight, target population, and domain_size

These checks decide whether the direct estimates are ready for area-level modelling, whether the estimator should be changed, or whether the estimand needs to be simplified.

title: Direct Estimation
text:
3 Next Step
The output needed for Area-level Modelling is:

domain_id
Direct, the survey-only estimate
Variance, the estimated sampling variance of direct







Note



sae::direct() returns core direct-estimation outputs by domain, including sample size (N), direct estimate (Direct), and standard deviation (SD), which can be squared to obtain Variance.


However, before proceeding, check whether the direct estimates are ready for area-level modelling:

domains with no sampled units
domains with very few valid units, or dominated by very few large weights
zero or extremely small variances
estimates outside the valid range for the indicator

The table below links common direct-estimate patterns to practical next steps.



Scenario
Next step




Most domains have usable sample sizes and uncertainty is acceptable
Direct estimates may be enough for reporting.


Too many domains have no sample or unusable variances
Revisit the domain choice or redefine estimand.


Estimates are noisy but variances are usable
Fit a covariate-based area-level SAE model.
